title: ‘Good design takes mastery’: scientific illustrators sketch out AI’s future
excerpt: Nature talks to five artists about their approaches to communicating science visually.
content_html:
By Anne Pichon
22 July 2026

Nature talks to five artists about their approaches to communicating science visually.

Artificial-intelligence tools are making it easier than ever to generate technical images. But how good are their outputs? Nature asked five illustrators what it takes to create an accurate, engaging image that conveys a scientific or medical concept.
Shehryar Saharan, a scientific illustrator, educator and researcher at the University of Toronto, Canada, investigates how AI technologies reshape creative judgement and agency. Kuukua Wilson, a Ghanaian–US medical illustrator and painter now based in Delft, the Netherlands, advocates for equity in health care. Júlia d’Oliveira, a biologist turned palaeo- and wildlife artist in São Carlos, Brazil, recreates extinct species in exquisite detail. Melissa Weiss, a graphic designer in San Diego, California, conveys ever-evolving concepts in space science. And Ella Marushchenko, a Russian-born artist now based in Charleston, South Carolina, draws from her experience in painting, design and photography to design journal covers for researchers across the scientific spectrum. They share their views on the future of scientific illustration.

Anatomical illustration by Kuukua Wilson of a knee segment showing the bones, ligaments and muscles of this complex joint.

Anatomical illustration by Kuukua Wilson of a knee segment showing the bones, ligaments and muscles of this complex joint.

What do people misunderstand about scientific illustration?

Saharan: People underestimate the labour behind an image. Good design takes mastery, not merely of tools and techniques, but also of judgement. The craft of a scientific illustrator lies in the design process: the detective work of gathering information, the synthesis and appraisal of disparate, and sometimes conflicting, sources and the wrestling of complex information into an image. This visual problem solving is a mode of inquiry as much as a mode of representation.

Sometimes that process means confronting questions that research has not yet resolved. In those moments, illustration can become a way of generating knowledge rather than simply communicating it.

Wilson: Everybody has experienced scientific illustration, through images in medical books, leaflets or a science magazine. Yet most people don’t think about where these come from.

A few years ago, I worked with the University of Texas to create visuals on wounds for medical doctors training in regions with low health literacy. These require simple visual-art techniques to explain when the severity of a wound changes, when to manage it with home care and when to go to the clinic.

This is as much about education as it is about illustration. The medical literature is quite complex; the illustrator breaks it down into images that are accessible to the person who needs them. They are often much easier to understand than text.

Sketches and final illustration of Turnersuchus hingleyae, a marine crocodilian from the Jurassic period, by Júlia d’Oliveira.

Sketches and final illustration of Turnersuchus hingleyae, a marine crocodilian from the Jurassic period, by Júlia d’Oliveira.

How do you approach a project?

Wilson: I always start with research to familiarize myself with the project. I create small sketches, to work out the general look and feel of the visuals. Then I have a back and forth with the client. Their input is important, because I am not a medical professional. It’s a collaborative process.

Saharan: Absolutely, the collaboration is incredibly important. We don’t just create a beautiful image, that’s not the substance of the work.

Every project starts from curiosity and sustained enquiry. If I’m visualizing a surgical sequence, for example, I gather everything I can find — papers, footage, anatomical references. If possible, I go into the operating room to observe the procedure. Then I work through rounds and rounds of iteration with specialists in the field.

In our line of work, you’ve got to set your ego aside, confront your biases and assumptions and question them constantly. You become an investigator who is always learning things. You work closely not just with scientific specialists but also with the audience the image is for. The best illustration work is first and foremost accurate and it addresses a specific communication challenge. When that rigour meets beautiful, well-considered design, that’s where the real power lies.

A series of images showing scientific illustrations showing two detailed prehistoric marine reptile., image

d’Oliveira: Artists make things come alive. I draw extinct species: having a biology background helps a lot. I need to know how evolution works and how phylogenetic relationships in terms of evolutionary connections and lineages work. To reconstruct a dinosaur, for example, I use not just information from fossils but also comparisons with its closest living relatives: birds and crocodiles. That will inform aspects of the dinosaur’s appearance, such as the presence and shape of scales and patterns. Then I need to fill in the gaps in an educated way. I also read a lot about geology to imagine the environment the animal lived in. And I write down every detail relevant to the illustration so I can refer back to it — like references for a scientific paper.

Because I come from a scientific background I know geologists, biologists and palaeontologists who can give me insights into their respective areas. With each job, I learn something new.

Weiss: Knowing how to talk to scientists is crucial. I am not an astronomer; each concept includes some visual element that is new to me. It’s important to ask a lot of questions even, when it feels uncomfortable. If I don’t understand what I’m illustrating, how will I help others to do so?

Advances in understanding astronomical phenomena also affects how I visualize cosmic events and objects. For example, as astrophysicists gain insight into gravitational lensing (the bending of light around a massive celestial object), I can create more-thoughtful renderings of black holes.

In the distant Universe, some infant galaxies contain black holes that are more massive than expected from the size of their host galaxies. Melissa Weiss portrays that size difference by showing how a black hole (centre) would look inside a small distant galaxy (left) as well as a larger nearby one (right).

Credit: CfA/Melissa Weiss

A series of four digital art workspace screenshots documenting the development of a black hole illustration, from an initial structure and composition to a completed rendering embedded within a spiral galaxy., image

Has AI affected your work?

Saharan: The work hasn’t changed as much as the dialogue around AI would suggest — there’s an ‘adopt or die’ energy in a lot of professional discourse right now. I’m sceptical of both extremes.

Marushchenko: For me, it has changed what scientists bring to kick-start a project. When I started, it would be a hand-drawn sketch. That sketch was a direct line into how a researcher thought about their work. Today, it is often an AI-generated image, saturated, heavily lit, technically confident looking — and sometimes scientifically wrong.

AI has changed what clients expect. The AI aesthetic has become a default visual reference point that many researchers now push towards. But we often create images for scientific journals. When clients ask us to chase the AI look, we explain that it is likely to work against them as it doesn’t match what journals want: clarity over spectacle. Sometimes they listen.

Júlia d’Oliveira wanted to show how bird skulls and brains evolved by using images derived from fossil descriptions (left and middle) and of a living bird (right), first as a sketch and then in its final rendering. 

A series of images showing three bird heads from different evolutionary stages, with cutaway views revealing enlarged and increasingly complex brain structures., image

The images are based on the extinct Archaeopteryx (left) and Navaornis (middle) as well as a bird from the Tangara genus (right).

Credit: Júlia d'Oliveira

Do you use AI tools and, if so, how?

Weiss: When AI was first introduced as a source of image making, its inaccuracies took just as long to correct as it would to start from scratch. And, by using it, I lost part of my authority over the end result. I still think that this is a major issue when using an AI tool to produce an original, scientifically accurate image.

Many AI tools are now being integrated into the software I use to create visualizations. I use them just as I would a brush or an eraser, to remove small artefacts or increase the background area by a few centimetres — not to build the main base of an image. This level of use maintains my agency over the work and avoids concerns about accuracy or copyright infringement.

Saharan: Yes, AI is entangled not only with ethical concerns, but also with questions of copyright, authorship, accountability and consent around training data. The images I create often communicate information that is sensitive, consequential or has not been visualized adequately before. That kind of work carries a level of responsibility that a generative AI tool cannot assume on your behalf.

I explore AI systems across the production pipeline — from research and ideation to visual production — to understand what they can and can’t do. But, so far, generative-AI tools haven’t entered my finished work. That’s not a blanket refusal, it’s a judgement call that I keep revisiting as the tools and their provenance evolve.

Marushchenko: I’m in a similar situation. In the studio I founded, we do not use AI tools in our design process. Our team includes PhD-level scientists who verify that every structural detail depicted is accurate.

d’Oliveira: In testing how AI tools work, I found that they are very good at rendering lights and shadows that look realistic. But they miss the mark on perspective, foreground and background, as well as accuracy. Ask an AI tool to recreate an animal, such as a dinosaur, from its skeleton and it will generate what it was trained on: a lot of bad art. It will also gather information from incorrect sources.

Wilson: I, too, make a conscious effort to not use any AI tools in illustrating or design. I am trying them out at the early research stages of a project when I’m gathering information. AI can help to seek out sources. But even there, I’m more comfortable defaulting to search engines such as Google Scholar to locate peer-reviewed papers.

A flying reptile known as a pterosaur (Tapejara wellnhoferi) depicted by Júlia d’Oliveira.

A flying reptile known as a pterosaur (Tapejara wellnhoferi) depicted by Júlia d’Oliveira.

Illustration by Júlia d’Oliveira of the Motuca Formation, Brazil, which preserves fossils of diverse flora, centred around a fern (Grammatopteris).

Credit: Júlia d’Oliveira

A detailed painting depicting a prehistoric forest scene with various ancient plants, including large tree ferns and tall conifer-like trees surrounding a calm water body. The artwork highlights lush vegetation and diverse plant structures., image

What do you think AI cannot grasp in scientific illustrations?

Marushchenko: An AI tool will generate an image of almost anything you describe to it, including concepts that do not yet exist in its training data. But it cannot verify whether what it produces is scientifically accurate. It fills in the gaps with visually plausible details that might be subtly or substantially wrong. Yet the result looks authoritative: that is precisely what makes it problematic.

Saharan: In scientific illustration, you make decisions about what is known, what is uncertain, what a particular audience needs and how to represent that visually. AI cannot make those choices and work with a clinician or researcher to negotiate what the image needs to communicate. In my view, those are deeply human, contextual, judgement-laden acts.

Take the female body, for example. There’s a lack of available work that stems from a long history of exclusion, neglect and uneven scientific attention. That history lives in the images, too.

This year, I worked on an anatomical illustration of clitoral and vulvar innervation — structures that have long been understudied. They have been resolved at high resolution and mapped only over the past year. Surgical procedures such as gender-affirming surgery and reconstructive care for survivors of female genital mutilation end up severing these nerves. So, it’s important to understand where they are. AI can’t help there, you can’t prompt your way through that.

Other gaps where medicine has historically overlooked certain bodies, experiences or communities include intersex anatomy, gender-affirming care, racial diversity, disability, chronic pain, rare diseases, ageing and the visual communication of invisible illnesses.

Wilson: We need to create images to show how a specific condition looks across a range of skin tones. People have gone to the doctor with symptoms and been wrongly dismissed because the physician hadn’t recognized their condition as a result of their skin tone.

I always start with brown skin, because I have brown skin. If I were to come across this disease, how would it look? Awareness has improved over the past few years, but implementation not so much. I don’t see it enough. For diseases that have topical symptoms, creating images in four or five different variations should be the default — not just something we do for one specific project.

And if AI is really to become a central tool, it would make sense to also teach the models to default to representative sources.

Do different fields require different approaches to illustrating?

Marushchenko: Absolutely. And that variety is a big part of why we enjoy the work. Each field forces you to think differently. In biology, a simple schematic is fine for a figure in a scientific paper, but for a cover, the hardest part is making cells and proteins look alive. You need the right textures, lighting and materials, otherwise everything looks flat and lifeless.

Chemistry and materials science are some of my favourite fields to work in. Many molecular structures are already known, so we have a solid foundation to build on. From there, my colleagues and I get to be creative about how we show reactions, molecular interactions or the behaviour of a material. Often, we are asked to visualize a material for which we have only a handful of microscopy images to work from. This means that we get to bring something to life that has barely been pictured before.

Theoretical physics brings a different kind of freedom. Many phenomena have no physical appearance at all, so we are not tied to reproducing something exactly. Instead, we design a visual metaphor that captures the idea.

In the past year, I have spent months with my team on a single figure for a neuroscience project. The subject was not a structure but a pattern: how the brain moves between states and how it can sit near a threshold and tip one way or the other. There was no anatomy to lean on, nothing to photograph — only a way of thinking drawn from dynamic systems, in which behaviour is described as a shifting landscape rather than as a set of fixed parts.

And what of the future? What would you tell budding illustrators?

d’Oliveira: I don’t think scientific illustrators are threatened by AI. I think it’s a bigger threat for non-scientific illustration.

Weiss: We must continue learning: as creative individuals, we need to continually push ourselves to improve. And build relationships. The connections you make are important in your career.

Saharan: There is certainly some catastrophizing — but I would say that students are appropriately worried. We don’t know where the technology is going.

Much of the concern focuses on what the models might one day do, but there are other aspects: the unresolved questions of copyright and ownership, the environmental costs of these tools and the costs to our craft and to our voice in the field.

I advise students to keep tabs on AI, to be aware. It takes discipline to resist seductive tools that promise ease and efficiency, and to ask instead what we risk losing when creating gets easy. To me, that is the real question: how to remain intentional.

Wilson: Yes, there is a humming concern, which I agree is reasonable.

When AI first came up, it was very much as: ‘this is going to replace you’. Many of us pushed it away immediately: something that will replace me, but first I need to feed it my work? Make it make sense.

At the moment, if someone wants to depict the correct anatomy, they still need a medical illustrator. And I often think about what a professor said during my master’s: people used to worry that illustrators would be replaced by the camera. But if a camera takes a photo of a surgery, you don’t see anything, just blood. You still need a medical illustrator to draw everything else.

I anticipate something similar happening in the next ten years: illustrators will still be needed to clarify, correct, modify and make images that various audiences can understand. Newcomers should be concerned, but more about learning enough about AI to be able to keep it in its place: it is just a tool.

These interviews have been edited for length and clarity.

Interviewer and editor: Anne Pichon

Interviewed illustrators: Shay Saharan, Júlia d’Oliveira, Ella Marushchenko, Kuukua Wilson, Melissa Weiss

Media editor: Agnese Abrusci

Subeditor: Francisca Schultz

Shehryar Saharan, a scientific illustrator, educator and researcher at the University of Toronto, Canada, investigates how AI technologies reshape creative judgement and agency. Kuukua Wilson, a Ghanaian–US medical illustrator and painter now based in Delft, the Netherlands, advocates for equity in health care. Júlia d’Oliveira, a biologist turned palaeo- and wildlife artist in São Carlos, Brazil, recreates extinct species in exquisite detail. Melissa Weiss, a graphic designer in San Diego, California, conveys ever-evolving concepts in space science. And Ella Marushchenko, a Russian-born artist now based in Charleston, South Carolina, draws from her experience in painting, design and photography to design journal covers for researchers across the scientific spectrum. They share their views on the future of scientific illustration.

sentences.en: Artificial-intelligence tools are making it easier than ever to generate technical images.
sentences.zh: 人工智能工具使生成技术图像变得比以往更容易。

sentences.en: But how good are their outputs?
sentences.zh: 但他们的产出有多好呢？

sentences.en: Nature asked five illustrators what it takes to create an accurate, engaging image that conveys a scientific or medical concept.Shehryar Saharan, a scientific illustrator, educator and researcher at the University of Toronto, Canada, investigates how AI technologies reshape creative judgement and agency.
sentences.zh: 《自然》杂志向五位插画家询问了如何才能创作出准确、引人入胜的图像来传达科学或医学概念。Shehryar Saharan 是加拿大多伦多大学的科学插画家、教育家和研究员，他研究人工智能技术如何重塑创造性判断和能动性。

sentences.en: Kuukua Wilson, a Ghanaian–US medical illustrator and painter now based in Delft, the Netherlands, advocates for equity in health care.

sentences.en: They share their views on the future of scientific illustration.

blocks.html_en: What do people misunderstand about scientific illustration?

sentences.en: What do people misunderstand about scientific illustration?
sentences.zh: 人们对科学插图有什么误解？

blocks.html_en: Saharan: People underestimate the labour behind an image. Good design takes mastery, not merely of tools and techniques, but also of judgement. The craft of a scientific illustrator lies in the design process: the detective work of gathering information, the synthesis and appraisal of disparate, and sometimes conflicting, sources and the wrestling of complex information into an image. This visual problem solving is a mode of inquiry as much as a mode of representation.

sentences.en: Saharan: People underestimate the labour behind an image.
sentences.zh: 撒哈拉：人们低估了图像背后的劳动。

sentences.en: Good design takes mastery, not merely of tools and techniques, but also of judgement.
sentences.zh: 好的设计不仅需要掌握工具和技术，还需要掌握判断力。

sentences.en: The craft of a scientific illustrator lies in the design process: the detective work of gathering information, the synthesis and appraisal of disparate, and sometimes conflicting, sources and the wrestling of complex information into an image.
sentences.zh: 科学插画家的技巧在于设计过程：收集信息的侦探工作、对不同的、有时甚至是相互冲突的来源的综合和评估，以及将复杂的信息转化为图像。

sentences.en: This visual problem solving is a mode of inquiry as much as a mode of representation.
sentences.zh: 这种视觉问题解决方式既是一种探究模式，也是一种表征模式。

blocks.html_en: Sometimes that process means confronting questions that research has not yet resolved. In those moments, illustration can become a way of generating knowledge rather than simply communicating it.

sentences.en: Sometimes that process means confronting questions that research has not yet resolved.
sentences.zh: 有时，这个过程意味着面对研究尚未解决的问题。

sentences.en: In those moments, illustration can become a way of generating knowledge rather than simply communicating it.
sentences.zh: 在那些时刻，插图可以成为一种生成知识的方式，而不仅仅是传达知识。

blocks.html_en: Wilson: Everybody has experienced scientific illustration, through images in medical books, leaflets or a science magazine. Yet most people don’t think about where these come from.

sentences.en: Wilson: Everybody has experienced scientific illustration, through images in medical books, leaflets or a science magazine.
sentences.zh: 威尔逊：每个人都通过医学书籍、传单或科学杂志中的图像体验过科学插图。

sentences.en: Yet most people don’t think about where these come from.
sentences.zh: 然而大多数人并没有考虑这些来自哪里。

blocks.html_en: A few years ago, I worked with the University of Texas to create visuals on wounds for medical doctors training in regions with low health literacy. These require simple visual-art techniques to explain when the severity of a wound changes, when to manage it with home care and when to go to the clinic.

sentences.en: A few years ago, I worked with the University of Texas to create visuals on wounds for medical doctors training in regions with low health literacy.
sentences.zh: 几年前，我与德克萨斯大学合作，为健康素养较低地区接受培训的医生制作伤口视觉效果。

sentences.en: These require simple visual-art techniques to explain when the severity of a wound changes, when to manage it with home care and when to go to the clinic.
sentences.zh: 这些需要简单的视觉艺术技术来解释伤口的严重程度何时发生变化、何时进行家庭护理以及何时去诊所。

blocks.html_en: This is as much about education as it is about illustration. The medical literature is quite complex; the illustrator breaks it down into images that are accessible to the person who needs them. They are often much easier to understand than text.

sentences.en: This is as much about education as it is about illustration.
sentences.zh: 这既与教育有关，也与插图有关。

sentences.en: The medical literature is quite complex; the illustrator breaks it down into images that are accessible to the person who needs them.
sentences.zh: 医学文献相当复杂；插画家将其分解为需要的人可以访问的图像。

sentences.en: They are often much easier to understand than text.
sentences.zh: 它们通常比文本更容易理解。

blocks.html_en: How do you approach a project?

sentences.en: How do you approach a project?
sentences.zh: 你如何处理一个项目？

blocks.html_en: Wilson: I always start with research to familiarize myself with the project. I create small sketches, to work out the general look and feel of the visuals. Then I have a back and forth with the client. Their input is important, because I am not a medical professional. It’s a collaborative process.

sentences.en: Wilson: I always start with research to familiarize myself with the project.
sentences.zh: Wilson：我总是从研究开始，以熟悉该项目。

sentences.en: I create small sketches, to work out the general look and feel of the visuals.
sentences.zh: 我创建小草图，以绘制出视觉效果的总体外观和感觉。

sentences.en: Then I have a back and forth with the client.
sentences.zh: 然后我和客户进行了一次来回的交流。

sentences.en: Their input is important, because I am not a medical professional.
sentences.zh: 他们的意见很重要，因为我不是医学专业人士。

sentences.en: It’s a collaborative process.
sentences.zh: 这是一个协作的过程。

blocks.html_en: Saharan: Absolutely, the collaboration is incredibly important. We don’t just create a beautiful image, that’s not the substance of the work.

sentences.en: Saharan: Absolutely, the collaboration is incredibly important.
sentences.zh: Saharan：当然，合作非常重要。

sentences.en: We don’t just create a beautiful image, that’s not the substance of the work.
sentences.zh: 我们不只是创造一个美丽的形象，那不是作品的实质。

blocks.html_en: Every project starts from curiosity and sustained enquiry. If I’m visualizing a surgical sequence, for example, I gather everything I can find — papers, footage, anatomical references. If possible, I go into the operating room to observe the procedure. Then I work through rounds and rounds of iteration with specialists in the field.

sentences.en: Every project starts from curiosity and sustained enquiry.
sentences.zh: 每个项目都始于好奇心和持续的探究。

sentences.en: If I’m visualizing a surgical sequence, for example, I gather everything I can find — papers, footage, anatomical references.
sentences.zh: 例如，如果我想象一个手术序列，我会收集我能找到的所有东西——论文、录像、解剖参考资料。

sentences.en: If possible, I go into the operating room to observe the procedure.
sentences.zh: 如果可以的话，我会去手术室观察手术过程。

sentences.en: Then I work through rounds and rounds of iteration with specialists in the field.
sentences.zh: 然后我与该领域的专家进行一轮又一轮的迭代。

blocks.html_en: In our line of work, you’ve got to set your ego aside, confront your biases and assumptions and question them constantly. You become an investigator who is always learning things. You work closely not just with scientific specialists but also with the audience the image is for. The best illustration work is first and foremost accurate and it addresses a specific communication challenge. When that rigour meets beautiful, well-considered design, that’s where the real power lies.

sentences.en: In our line of work, you’ve got to set your ego aside, confront your biases and assumptions and question them constantly.
sentences.zh: 在我们的工作中，你必须把你的自我放在一边，面对你的偏见和假设，并不断质疑它们。

sentences.en: You become an investigator who is always learning things.
sentences.zh: 你成为一名不断学习东西的调查员。

sentences.en: You work closely not just with scientific specialists but also with the audience the image is for.
sentences.zh: 您不仅与科学专家密切合作，还与图像的受众密切合作。

sentences.en: The best illustration work is first and foremost accurate and it addresses a specific communication challenge.
sentences.zh: 最好的插图作品首先是准确的，它解决了特定的沟通挑战。

sentences.en: When that rigour meets beautiful, well-considered design, that’s where the real power lies.
sentences.zh: 当严谨与精美、深思熟虑的设计相结合时，这就是真正的力量所在。

blocks.html_en: A series of images showing scientific illustrations showing two detailed prehistoric marine reptile., image

sentences.en: A series of images showing scientific illustrations showing two detailed prehistoric marine reptile., image
sentences.zh: 一系列显示科学插图的图像显示了两种详细的史前海洋爬行动物。，图像

blocks.html_en: d’Oliveira: Artists make things come alive. I draw extinct species: having a biology background helps a lot. I need to know how evolution works and how phylogenetic relationships in terms of evolutionary connections and lineages work. To reconstruct a dinosaur, for example, I use not just information from fossils but also comparisons with its closest living relatives: birds and crocodiles. That will inform aspects of the dinosaur’s appearance, such as the presence and shape of scales and patterns. Then I need to fill in the gaps in an educated way. I also read a lot about geology to imagine the environment the animal lived in. And I write down every detail relevant to the illustration so I can refer back to it — like references for a scientific paper.

sentences.en: d’Oliveira: Artists make things come alive.
sentences.zh: d’Oliveira：艺术家让事物变得生动起来。

sentences.en: I draw extinct species: having a biology background helps a lot.
sentences.zh: 我画灭绝的物种：拥有生物学背景有很大帮助。

sentences.en: I need to know how evolution works and how phylogenetic relationships in terms of evolutionary connections and lineages work.
sentences.zh: 我需要知道进化是如何运作的，以及进化联系和谱系方面的系统发育关系是如何运作的。

sentences.en: To reconstruct a dinosaur, for example, I use not just information from fossils but also comparisons with its closest living relatives: birds and crocodiles.
sentences.zh: 例如，为了重建恐龙，我不仅使用化石中的信息，还使用与其最近的现存亲戚：鸟类和鳄鱼进行比较。

sentences.en: That will inform aspects of the dinosaur’s appearance, such as the presence and shape of scales and patterns.
sentences.zh: 这将揭示恐龙外观的各个方面，例如鳞片和图案的存在和形状。

sentences.en: Then I need to fill in the gaps in an educated way.
sentences.zh: 然后我需要以受过教育的方式填补空白。

sentences.en: I also read a lot about geology to imagine the environment the animal lived in.
sentences.zh: 我还阅读了很多地质学知识来想象动物生活的环境。

sentences.en: And I write down every detail relevant to the illustration so I can refer back to it — like references for a scientific paper.
sentences.zh: 我写下与插图相关的每个细节，以便我可以回顾它 - 就像科学论文的参考文献一样。

blocks.html_en: Because I come from a scientific background I know geologists, biologists and palaeontologists who can give me insights into their respective areas. With each job, I learn something new.

sentences.en: Because I come from a scientific background I know geologists, biologists and palaeontologists who can give me insights into their respective areas.
sentences.zh: 因为我有科学背景，所以我认识地质学家、生物学家和古生物学家，他们可以让我深入了解各自的领域。

sentences.en: With each job, I learn something new.
sentences.zh: 每一份工作，我都会学到新的东西。

blocks.html_en: Weiss: Knowing how to talk to scientists is crucial. I am not an astronomer; each concept includes some visual element that is new to me. It’s important to ask a lot of questions even, when it feels uncomfortable. If I don’t understand what I’m illustrating, how will I help others to do so?

sentences.en: Weiss: Knowing how to talk to scientists is crucial.
sentences.zh: Weiss：知道如何与科学家交谈至关重要。

sentences.en: I am not an astronomer; each concept includes some visual element that is new to me.
sentences.zh: 我不是天文学家；每个概念都包含一些对我来说是新的视觉元素。

sentences.en: It’s important to ask a lot of questions even, when it feels uncomfortable.
sentences.zh: 即使在感觉不舒服的时候，提出很多问题也很重要。

sentences.en: If I don’t understand what I’m illustrating, how will I help others to do so?
sentences.zh: 如果我不明白我所说明的内容，我将如何帮助其他人这样做？

blocks.html_en: Advances in understanding astronomical phenomena also affects how I visualize cosmic events and objects. For example, as astrophysicists gain insight into gravitational lensing (the bending of light around a massive celestial object), I can create more-thoughtful renderings of black holes.

sentences.en: Advances in understanding astronomical phenomena also affects how I visualize cosmic events and objects.
sentences.zh: 对天文现象理解的进步也影响了我对宇宙事件和物体的可视化方式。

sentences.en: For example, as astrophysicists gain insight into gravitational lensing (the bending of light around a massive celestial object), I can create more-thoughtful renderings of black holes.
sentences.zh: 例如，当天体物理学家深入了解引力透镜（巨大天体周围光线的弯曲）时，我可以创建更周到的黑洞渲染。

blocks.html_en: In the distant Universe, some infant galaxies contain black holes that are more massive than expected from the size of their host galaxies. Melissa Weiss portrays that size difference by showing how a black hole (centre) would look inside a small distant galaxy (left) as well as a larger nearby one (right).

sentences.en: In the distant Universe, some infant galaxies contain black holes that are more massive than expected from the size of their host galaxies.
sentences.zh: 在遥远的宇宙中，一些婴儿星系含有黑洞，其质量比根据其宿主星系的大小所预期的要大。

sentences.en: Melissa Weiss portrays that size difference by showing how a black hole (centre) would look inside a small distant galaxy (left) as well as a larger nearby one (right).
sentences.zh: 梅丽莎·韦斯（Melissa Weiss）通过展示黑洞（中）在遥远的小星系（左）和附近较大的星系（右）内的外观来描绘这种大小差异。

blocks.html_en: Has AI affected your work?

sentences.en: Has AI affected your work?
sentences.zh: AI对你的工作有影响吗？

blocks.html_en: Saharan: The work hasn’t changed as much as the dialogue around AI would suggest — there’s an ‘adopt or die’ energy in a lot of professional discourse right now. I’m sceptical of both extremes.

sentences.en: Saharan: The work hasn’t changed as much as the dialogue around AI would suggest — there’s an ‘adopt or die’ energy in a lot of professional discourse right now.
sentences.zh: Saharan：这项工作并没有像围绕人工智能的对话所暗示的那样发生太大变化——现在很多专业讨论中有一种“采用或死亡”的能量。

sentences.en: I’m sceptical of both extremes.
sentences.zh: 我对这两个极端都持怀疑态度。

blocks.html_en: Marushchenko: For me, it has changed what scientists bring to kick-start a project. When I started, it would be a hand-drawn sketch. That sketch was a direct line into how a researcher thought about their work. Today, it is often an AI-generated image, saturated, heavily lit, technically confident looking — and sometimes scientifically wrong.

sentences.en: Marushchenko: For me, it has changed what scientists bring to kick-start a project.
sentences.zh: Marushchenko：对我来说，它改变了科学家启动项目的方式。

sentences.en: When I started, it would be a hand-drawn sketch.
sentences.zh: 当我开始时，这将是一个手绘草图。

sentences.en: That sketch was a direct line into how a researcher thought about their work.
sentences.zh: 这张草图直接反映了研究人员如何看待他们的工作。

sentences.en: Today, it is often an AI-generated image, saturated, heavily lit, technically confident looking — and sometimes scientifically wrong.
sentences.zh: 如今，它通常是人工智能生成的图像，饱和、光线充足、技术上看起来很自信，但有时在科学上是错误的。

blocks.html_en: AI has changed what clients expect. The AI aesthetic has become a default visual reference point that many researchers now push towards. But we often create images for scientific journals. When clients ask us to chase the AI look, we explain that it is likely to work against them as it doesn’t match what journals want: clarity over spectacle. Sometimes they listen.

sentences.en: AI has changed what clients expect.
sentences.zh: 人工智能改变了客户的期望。

sentences.en: The AI aesthetic has become a default visual reference point that many researchers now push towards.
sentences.zh: 人工智能美学已经成为许多研究人员现在推动的默认视觉参考点。

sentences.en: But we often create images for scientific journals.
sentences.zh: 但我们经常为科学期刊创建图像。

sentences.en: When clients ask us to chase the AI look, we explain that it is likely to work against them as it doesn’t match what journals want: clarity over spectacle.
sentences.zh: 当客户要求我们追求人工智能外观时，我们解释说这可能会对他们不利，因为它不符合期刊的要求：清晰度胜过奇观。

sentences.en: Sometimes they listen.
sentences.zh: 有时他们会听。

blocks.html_en: Júlia d’Oliveira wanted to show how bird skulls and brains evolved by using images derived from fossil descriptions (left and middle) and of a living bird (right), first as a sketch and then in its final rendering.

sentences.en: Júlia d’Oliveira wanted to show how bird skulls and brains evolved by using images derived from fossil descriptions (left and middle) and of a living bird (right), first as a sketch and then in its final rendering.
sentences.zh: Júlia d’Oliveira 希望通过使用来自化石描述（左和中）和活鸟（右）的图像来展示鸟类头骨和大脑是如何进化的，首先是草图，然后是最终的渲染。

blocks.html_en: Do you use AI tools and, if so, how?

sentences.en: Do you use AI tools and, if so, how?
sentences.zh: 您是否使用人工智能工具？如果使用，如何使用？

blocks.html_en: Weiss: When AI was first introduced as a source of image making, its inaccuracies took just as long to correct as it would to start from scratch. And, by using it, I lost part of my authority over the end result. I still think that this is a major issue when using an AI tool to produce an original, scientifically accurate image.

sentences.en: Weiss: When AI was first introduced as a source of image making, its inaccuracies took just as long to correct as it would to start from scratch.
sentences.zh: Weiss：当人工智能首次作为图像制作的来源被引入时，它的不准确性需要与从头开始一样长的时间来纠正。

sentences.en: And, by using it, I lost part of my authority over the end result.
sentences.zh: 而且，通过使用它，我失去了对最终结果的部分权威。

sentences.en: I still think that this is a major issue when using an AI tool to produce an original, scientifically accurate image.
sentences.zh: 我仍然认为，在使用人工智能工具生成原始的、科学上准确的图像时，这是一个主要问题。

blocks.html_en: Many AI tools are now being integrated into the software I use to create visualizations. I use them just as I would a brush or an eraser, to remove small artefacts or increase the background area by a few centimetres — not to build the main base of an image. This level of use maintains my agency over the work and avoids concerns about accuracy or copyright infringement.

sentences.en: Many AI tools are now being integrated into the software I use to create visualizations.
sentences.zh: 许多人工智能工具现在被集成到我用来创建可视化的软件中。

sentences.en: I use them just as I would a brush or an eraser, to remove small artefacts or increase the background area by a few centimetres — not to build the main base of an image.
sentences.zh: 我使用它们就像使用画笔或橡皮擦一样，用于去除小伪影或将背景区域增加几厘米，而不是构建图像的主要基础。

sentences.en: This level of use maintains my agency over the work and avoids concerns about accuracy or copyright infringement.
sentences.zh: 这种程度的使用可以维持我对作品的代理权，并避免对准确性或侵犯版权的担忧。

blocks.html_en: Saharan: Yes, AI is entangled not only with ethical concerns, but also with questions of copyright, authorship, accountability and consent around training data. The images I create often communicate information that is sensitive, consequential or has not been visualized adequately before. That kind of work carries a level of responsibility that a generative AI tool cannot assume on your behalf.

sentences.en: Saharan: Yes, AI is entangled not only with ethical concerns, but also with questions of copyright, authorship, accountability and consent around training data.
sentences.zh: Saharan：是的，人工智能不仅与道德问题纠缠在一起，还与训练数据的版权、作者身份、责任和同意问题纠缠在一起。

sentences.en: The images I create often communicate information that is sensitive, consequential or has not been visualized adequately before.
sentences.zh: 我创建的图像经常传达敏感的、重要的或以前没有被充分可视化的信息。

sentences.en: That kind of work carries a level of responsibility that a generative AI tool cannot assume on your behalf.
sentences.zh: 这种工作所承担的责任是生成式人工智能工具无法代表你承担的。

blocks.html_en: I explore AI systems across the production pipeline — from research and ideation to visual production — to understand what they can and can’t do. But, so far, generative-AI tools haven’t entered my finished work. That’s not a blanket refusal, it’s a judgement call that I keep revisiting as the tools and their provenance evolve.

sentences.en: I explore AI systems across the production pipeline — from research and ideation to visual production — to understand what they can and can’t do.
sentences.zh: 我探索整个制作流程中的人工智能系统——从研究和构思到视觉制作——以了解它们能做什么和不能做什么。

sentences.en: But, so far, generative-AI tools haven’t entered my finished work.
sentences.zh: 但是，到目前为止，生成式人工智能工具还没有进入我完成的作品中。

sentences.en: That’s not a blanket refusal, it’s a judgement call that I keep revisiting as the tools and their provenance evolve.
sentences.zh: 这并不是全然拒绝，而是随着工具及其来源的发展，我不断重新审视的判断。

blocks.html_en: Marushchenko: I’m in a similar situation. In the studio I founded, we do not use AI tools in our design process. Our team includes PhD-level scientists who verify that every structural detail depicted is accurate.

sentences.en: Marushchenko: I’m in a similar situation.
sentences.zh: Marushchenko：我也有类似的情况。

sentences.en: In the studio I founded, we do not use AI tools in our design process.
sentences.zh: 在我创立的工作室中，我们在设计过程中不使用人工智能工具。

sentences.en: Our team includes PhD-level scientists who verify that every structural detail depicted is accurate.
sentences.zh: 我们的团队包括博士级科学家，他们验证所描述的每个结构细节都是准确的。

blocks.html_en: d’Oliveira: In testing how AI tools work, I found that they are very good at rendering lights and shadows that look realistic. But they miss the mark on perspective, foreground and background, as well as accuracy. Ask an AI tool to recreate an animal, such as a dinosaur, from its skeleton and it will generate what it was trained on: a lot of bad art. It will also gather information from incorrect sources.

sentences.en: d’Oliveira: In testing how AI tools work, I found that they are very good at rendering lights and shadows that look realistic.
sentences.zh: d’Oliveira：在测试人工智能工具的工作原理时，我发现它们非常擅长渲染看起来逼真的灯光和阴影。

sentences.en: But they miss the mark on perspective, foreground and background, as well as accuracy.
sentences.zh: 但他们在视角、前景和背景以及准确性方面没有达到目标。

sentences.en: Ask an AI tool to recreate an animal, such as a dinosaur, from its skeleton and it will generate what it was trained on: a lot of bad art.
sentences.zh: 让人工智能工具从骨骼中重新创造出一种动物，比如恐龙，它会生成它所接受的训练：大量糟糕的艺术作品。

sentences.en: It will also gather information from incorrect sources.
sentences.zh: 它还会从不正确的来源收集信息。

blocks.html_en: Wilson: I, too, make a conscious effort to not use any AI tools in illustrating or design. I am trying them out at the early research stages of a project when I’m gathering information. AI can help to seek out sources. But even there, I’m more comfortable defaulting to search engines such as Google Scholar to locate peer-reviewed papers.

sentences.en: Wilson: I, too, make a conscious effort to not use any AI tools in illustrating or design.
sentences.zh: Wilson：我也有意识地努力不在插图或设计中使用任何人工智能工具。

sentences.en: I am trying them out at the early research stages of a project when I’m gathering information.
sentences.zh: 当我收集信息时，我会在项目的早期研究阶段尝试它们。

sentences.en: AI can help to seek out sources.
sentences.zh: 人工智能可以帮助寻找资源。

sentences.en: But even there, I’m more comfortable defaulting to search engines such as Google Scholar to locate peer-reviewed papers.
sentences.zh: 但即便如此，我还是更愿意默认使用谷歌学术等搜索引擎来查找同行评审的论文。

blocks.html_en: What do you think AI cannot grasp in scientific illustrations?

sentences.en: What do you think AI cannot grasp in scientific illustrations?
sentences.zh: 您认为人工智能无法理解科学插图中的哪些内容？

blocks.html_en: Marushchenko: An AI tool will generate an image of almost anything you describe to it, including concepts that do not yet exist in its training data. But it cannot verify whether what it produces is scientifically accurate. It fills in the gaps with visually plausible details that might be subtly or substantially wrong. Yet the result looks authoritative: that is precisely what makes it problematic.

sentences.en: Marushchenko: An AI tool will generate an image of almost anything you describe to it, including concepts that do not yet exist in its training data.
sentences.zh: Marushchenko：人工智能工具将生成几乎所有你向其描述的内容的图像，包括其训练数据中尚不存在的概念。

sentences.en: But it cannot verify whether what it produces is scientifically accurate.
sentences.zh: 但它无法验证其产生的结果是否科学准确。

sentences.en: It fills in the gaps with visually plausible details that might be subtly or substantially wrong.
sentences.zh: 它用视觉上合理的细节填补了空白，而这些细节可能是微妙或实质上错误的。

sentences.en: Yet the result looks authoritative: that is precisely what makes it problematic.
sentences.zh: 然而结果看起来很权威：这正是它产生问题的原因。

blocks.html_en: Saharan: In scientific illustration, you make decisions about what is known, what is uncertain, what a particular audience needs and how to represent that visually. AI cannot make those choices and work with a clinician or researcher to negotiate what the image needs to communicate. In my view, those are deeply human, contextual, judgement-laden acts.

sentences.en: Saharan: In scientific illustration, you make decisions about what is known, what is uncertain, what a particular audience needs and how to represent that visually.
sentences.zh: 撒哈拉：在科学插图中，你要对已知的内容、不确定的内容、特定受众的需求以及如何以视觉方式表示这些需求做出决定。

sentences.en: AI cannot make those choices and work with a clinician or researcher to negotiate what the image needs to communicate.
sentences.zh: 人工智能无法做出这些选择，也无法与临床医生或研究人员合作来协商图像需要传达的内容。

sentences.en: In my view, those are deeply human, contextual, judgement-laden acts.
sentences.zh: 在我看来，这些都是非常人性化、符合情境、充满判断力的行为。

blocks.html_en: Take the female body, for example. There’s a lack of available work that stems from a long history of exclusion, neglect and uneven scientific attention. That history lives in the images, too.

sentences.en: Take the female body, for example.
sentences.zh: 以女性身体为例。

sentences.en: There’s a lack of available work that stems from a long history of exclusion, neglect and uneven scientific attention.
sentences.zh: 由于长期的排斥、忽视和科学关注不均，可用工作的缺乏。

sentences.en: That history lives in the images, too.
sentences.zh: 这段历史也存在于图像中。

blocks.html_en: This year, I worked on an anatomical illustration of clitoral and vulvar innervation — structures that have long been understudied. They have been resolved at high resolution and mapped only over the past year. Surgical procedures such as gender-affirming surgery and reconstructive care for survivors of female genital mutilation end up severing these nerves. So, it’s important to understand where they are. AI can’t help there, you can’t prompt your way through that.

sentences.en: This year, I worked on an anatomical illustration of clitoral and vulvar innervation — structures that have long been understudied.
sentences.zh: 今年，我研究了阴蒂和外阴神经支配的解剖学插图——这些结构长期以来一直未被充分研究。

sentences.en: They have been resolved at high resolution and mapped only over the past year.
sentences.zh: 它们是在过去一年才以高分辨率解析并绘制的。

sentences.en: Surgical procedures such as gender-affirming surgery and reconstructive care for survivors of female genital mutilation end up severing these nerves.
sentences.zh: 对女性生殖器残割幸存者进行的性别确认手术和重建护理等外科手术最终会切断这些神经。

sentences.en: So, it’s important to understand where they are.
sentences.zh: 因此，了解它们在哪里很重要。

sentences.en: AI can’t help there, you can’t prompt your way through that.
sentences.zh: 人工智能对此无能为力，你无法提示你解决这个问题。

blocks.html_en: Other gaps where medicine has historically overlooked certain bodies, experiences or communities include intersex anatomy, gender-affirming care, racial diversity, disability, chronic pain, rare diseases, ageing and the visual communication of invisible illnesses.

sentences.en: Other gaps where medicine has historically overlooked certain bodies, experiences or communities include intersex anatomy, gender-affirming care, racial diversity, disability, chronic pain, rare diseases, ageing and the visual communication of invisible illnesses.
sentences.zh: 医学历史上忽视某些身体、经历或社区的其他差距包括双性解剖学、性别肯定护理、种族多样性、残疾、慢性疼痛、罕见疾病、衰老和隐形疾病的视觉传达。

blocks.html_en: Wilson: We need to create images to show how a specific condition looks across a range of skin tones. People have gone to the doctor with symptoms and been wrongly dismissed because the physician hadn’t recognized their condition as a result of their skin tone.

sentences.en: Wilson: We need to create images to show how a specific condition looks across a range of skin tones.
sentences.zh: Wilson：我们需要创建图像来展示特定条件在一系列肤色上的表现。

sentences.en: People have gone to the doctor with symptoms and been wrongly dismissed because the physician hadn’t recognized their condition as a result of their skin tone.
sentences.zh: 人们带着症状去看医生，却被错误地拒绝了，因为医生没有根据他们的肤色识别出他们的病情。

sentences.en: Illustration of a cross-section of the skin by Kuukua Wilson.
sentences.zh: Kuukua Wilson 绘制的皮肤横截面插图。

sentences.en: Illustration of a cross-section of the skin by Kuukua Wilson.

sentences.en: Illustration of a cross-section of the skin by Kuukua Wilson.
sentences.zh: Kuukua Wilson 绘制的皮肤横截面插图。

blocks.html_en: I always start with brown skin, because I have brown skin. If I were to come across this disease, how would it look? Awareness has improved over the past few years, but implementation not so much. I don’t see it enough. For diseases that have topical symptoms, creating images in four or five different variations should be the default — not just something we do for one specific project.

sentences.en: I always start with brown skin, because I have brown skin.
sentences.zh: 我总是从棕色皮肤开始，因为我有棕色皮肤。

sentences.en: If I were to come across this disease, how would it look?
sentences.zh: 如果我遇到这种病，会是什么样子？

sentences.en: Awareness has improved over the past few years, but implementation not so much.
sentences.zh: 过去几年，人们的意识有所提高，但实施情况却不尽如人意。

sentences.en: I don’t see it enough.
sentences.zh: 我还看不够。

sentences.en: For diseases that have topical symptoms, creating images in four or five different variations should be the default — not just something we do for one specific project.
sentences.zh: 对于具有局部症状的疾病，创建四到五种不同变体的图像应该是默认的——而不仅仅是我们为一个特定项目所做的事情。

blocks.html_en: And if AI is really to become a central tool, it would make sense to also teach the models to default to representative sources.

sentences.en: And if AI is really to become a central tool, it would make sense to also teach the models to default to representative sources.
sentences.zh: 如果人工智能真的要成为一种核心工具，那么教会模型默认使用代表性来源也是有意义的。

blocks.html_en: Do different fields require different approaches to illustrating?

sentences.en: Do different fields require different approaches to illustrating?
sentences.zh: 不同的领域需要不同的阐释方法吗？

blocks.html_en: Marushchenko: Absolutely. And that variety is a big part of why we enjoy the work. Each field forces you to think differently. In biology, a simple schematic is fine for a figure in a scientific paper, but for a cover, the hardest part is making cells and proteins look alive. You need the right textures, lighting and materials, otherwise everything looks flat and lifeless.

sentences.en: Marushchenko: Absolutely.
sentences.zh: 马鲁申科：当然。

sentences.en: And that variety is a big part of why we enjoy the work.
sentences.zh: 这种多样性是我们享受这份工作的重要原因。

sentences.en: Each field forces you to think differently.
sentences.zh: 每个领域都迫使你以不同的方式思考。

sentences.en: In biology, a simple schematic is fine for a figure in a scientific paper, but for a cover, the hardest part is making cells and proteins look alive.
sentences.zh: 在生物学中，对于科学论文中的人物来说，简单的示意图就可以了，但对于封面来说，最难的部分是让细胞和蛋白质看起来栩栩如生。

sentences.en: You need the right textures, lighting and materials, otherwise everything looks flat and lifeless.
sentences.zh: 你需要正确的纹理、灯光和材料，否则一切看起来都是平淡无奇的。

blocks.html_en: Chemistry and materials science are some of my favourite fields to work in. Many molecular structures are already known, so we have a solid foundation to build on. From there, my colleagues and I get to be creative about how we show reactions, molecular interactions or the behaviour of a material. Often, we are asked to visualize a material for which we have only a handful of microscopy images to work from. This means that we get to bring something to life that has barely been pictured before.

sentences.en: Chemistry and materials science are some of my favourite fields to work in.
sentences.zh: 化学和材料科学是我最喜欢从事的领域之一。

sentences.en: Many molecular structures are already known, so we have a solid foundation to build on.
sentences.zh: 许多分子结构是已知的，因此我们拥有坚实的基础。

sentences.en: From there, my colleagues and I get to be creative about how we show reactions, molecular interactions or the behaviour of a material.
sentences.zh: 从那时起，我和我的同事们开始对如何显示反应、分子相互作用或材料行为发挥创意。

sentences.en: Often, we are asked to visualize a material for which we have only a handful of microscopy images to work from.
sentences.zh: 通常，我们被要求可视化一种材料，而我们只有少量的显微镜图像可供使用。

sentences.en: This means that we get to bring something to life that has barely been pictured before.
sentences.zh: 这意味着我们可以将以前从未想象过的东西变为现实。

blocks.html_en: Theoretical physics brings a different kind of freedom. Many phenomena have no physical appearance at all, so we are not tied to reproducing something exactly. Instead, we design a visual metaphor that captures the idea.

sentences.en: Theoretical physics brings a different kind of freedom.
sentences.zh: 理论物理学带来了一种不同的自由。

sentences.en: Many phenomena have no physical appearance at all, so we are not tied to reproducing something exactly.
sentences.zh: 许多现象根本没有物理外观，因此我们不必精确地再现某些东西。

sentences.en: Instead, we design a visual metaphor that captures the idea.
sentences.zh: 相反，我们设计了一个视觉隐喻来捕捉这个想法。

blocks.html_en: In the past year, I have spent months with my team on a single figure for a neuroscience project. The subject was not a structure but a pattern: how the brain moves between states and how it can sit near a threshold and tip one way or the other. There was no anatomy to lean on, nothing to photograph — only a way of thinking drawn from dynamic systems, in which behaviour is described as a shifting landscape rather than as a set of fixed parts.

sentences.en: In the past year, I have spent months with my team on a single figure for a neuroscience project.
sentences.zh: 在过去的一年里，我和我的团队花了几个月的时间为一个神经科学项目研究一个图形。

sentences.en: The subject was not a structure but a pattern: how the brain moves between states and how it can sit near a threshold and tip one way or the other.
sentences.zh: 主题不是一种结构，而是一种模式：大脑如何在状态之间移动，以及它如何位于阈值附近并朝某个方向倾斜。

sentences.en: There was no anatomy to lean on, nothing to photograph — only a way of thinking drawn from dynamic systems, in which behaviour is described as a shifting landscape rather than as a set of fixed parts.
sentences.zh: 没有解剖学可以依靠，没有什么可以拍摄——只有一种从动态系统中得出的思维方式，其中行为被描述为不断变化的景观，而不是一组固定的部分。

blocks.html_en: And what of the future? What would you tell budding illustrators?

sentences.en: And what of the future?
sentences.zh: 未来又如何呢？

sentences.en: What would you tell budding illustrators?
sentences.zh: 您想对崭露头角的插画家说些什么？

blocks.html_en: d’Oliveira: I don’t think scientific illustrators are threatened by AI. I think it’s a bigger threat for non-scientific illustration.

sentences.en: d’Oliveira: I don’t think scientific illustrators are threatened by AI.
sentences.zh: d’Oliveira：我不认为科学插画家受到人工智能的威胁。

sentences.en: I think it’s a bigger threat for non-scientific illustration.
sentences.zh: 我认为这对非科学插图来说是一个更大的威胁。

blocks.html_en: Weiss: We must continue learning: as creative individuals, we need to continually push ourselves to improve. And build relationships. The connections you make are important in your career.

sentences.en: Weiss: We must continue learning: as creative individuals, we need to continually push ourselves to improve.
sentences.zh: Weiss：我们必须继续学习：作为有创造力的个体，我们需要不断地推动自己进步。

sentences.en: And build relationships.
sentences.zh: 并建立关系。

sentences.en: The connections you make are important in your career.
sentences.zh: 您建立的联系对您的职业生涯非常重要。

blocks.html_en: Saharan: There is certainly some catastrophizing — but I would say that students are appropriately worried. We don’t know where the technology is going.

sentences.en: Saharan: There is certainly some catastrophizing — but I would say that students are appropriately worried.
sentences.zh: 撒哈拉：确实存在一些灾难性的情况，但我想说学生们的担心是适当的。

sentences.en: We don’t know where the technology is going.
sentences.zh: 我们不知道技术将走向何方。

blocks.html_en: Much of the concern focuses on what the models might one day do, but there are other aspects: the unresolved questions of copyright and ownership, the environmental costs of these tools and the costs to our craft and to our voice in the field.

sentences.en: Much of the concern focuses on what the models might one day do, but there are other aspects: the unresolved questions of copyright and ownership, the environmental costs of these tools and the costs to our craft and to our voice in the field.
sentences.zh: 大多数关注点集中在模型有一天可能会做什么，但还有其他方面：尚未解决的版权和所有权问题、这些工具的环境成本以及我们的工艺和我们在该领域的发言权的成本。

blocks.html_en: I advise students to keep tabs on AI, to be aware. It takes discipline to resist seductive tools that promise ease and efficiency, and to ask instead what we risk losing when creating gets easy. To me, that is the real question: how to remain intentional.

sentences.en: I advise students to keep tabs on AI, to be aware.
sentences.zh: 我建议学生密切关注人工智能，保持警惕。

sentences.en: It takes discipline to resist seductive tools that promise ease and efficiency, and to ask instead what we risk losing when creating gets easy.
sentences.zh: 需要纪律来抵制那些承诺简单和高效的诱人工具，并反问当创造变得容易时我们可能会失去什么。

sentences.en: To me, that is the real question: how to remain intentional.
sentences.zh: 对我来说，这才是真正的问题：如何保持有意向。

blocks.html_en: Wilson: Yes, there is a humming concern, which I agree is reasonable.

sentences.en: Wilson: Yes, there is a humming concern, which I agree is reasonable.
sentences.zh: 威尔逊：是的，有一种嗡嗡声的担忧，我认为这是合理的。

blocks.html_en: When AI first came up, it was very much as: ‘this is going to replace you’. Many of us pushed it away immediately: something that will replace me, but first I need to feed it my work? Make it make sense.

sentences.en: When AI first came up, it was very much as: ‘this is going to replace you’.
sentences.zh: 当人工智能第一次出现时，它就像是：“这将取代你”。

sentences.en: Many of us pushed it away immediately: something that will replace me, but first I need to feed it my work?
sentences.zh: 我们中的许多人立即把它推开了：它会取代我，但首先我需要为它提供我的工作？

sentences.en: Make it make sense.
sentences.zh: 让它有意义。

blocks.html_en: At the moment, if someone wants to depict the correct anatomy, they still need a medical illustrator. And I often think about what a professor said during my master’s: people used to worry that illustrators would be replaced by the camera. But if a camera takes a photo of a surgery, you don’t see anything, just blood. You still need a medical illustrator to draw everything else.

sentences.en: At the moment, if someone wants to depict the correct anatomy, they still need a medical illustrator.
sentences.zh: 目前，如果有人想要描绘出正确的解剖结构，他们仍然需要一名医学插画家。

sentences.en: And I often think about what a professor said during my master’s: people used to worry that illustrators would be replaced by the camera.
sentences.zh: 我经常想起硕士期间一位教授说过的话：人们曾经担心插画师会被相机取代。

sentences.zh: 但如果相机拍摄手术照片，你什么也看不到，只有血​​。

blocks.html_en: I anticipate something similar happening in the next ten years: illustrators will still be needed to clarify, correct, modify and make images that various audiences can understand. Newcomers should be concerned, but more about learning enough about AI to be able to keep it in its place: it is just a tool.

sentences.en: Newcomers should be concerned, but more about learning enough about AI to be able to keep it in its place: it is just a tool.

content_html_zh:
作者：安妮·皮尚 2026 年 7 月 22 日

《自然》杂志采访了五位艺术家，谈论他们以视觉方式传达科学的方法。

人工智能工具使生成技术图像变得比以往更容易。 但他们的产出有多好呢？ 《自然》杂志向五位插画家询问了如何才能创作出准确、引人入胜的图像来传达科学或医学概念。Shehryar Saharan 是加拿大多伦多大学的科学插画家、教育家和研究员，他研究人工智能技术如何重塑创造性判断和能动性。 Kuukua Wilson 是一位加纳裔美国医学插画家和画家，现居荷兰代尔夫特，倡导医疗保健公平。 Júlia d’Oliveira 是巴西圣卡洛斯的一位生物学家，后来成为古生物和野生动物艺术家，她以精致的细节再现了灭绝的物种。 梅丽莎·韦斯 (Melissa Weiss) 是加利福尼亚州圣地亚哥的一名平面设计师，她传达了不断发展的空间科学概念。 Ella Marushchenko 是一位俄罗斯出生的艺术家，现居南卡罗来纳州查尔斯顿，她借鉴自己在绘画、设计和摄影方面的经验，为各个科学领域的研究人员设计期刊封面。 他们分享了对科学插画未来的看法。

Kuukua Wilson 绘制的膝关节解剖图显示了这个复杂关节的骨骼、韧带和肌肉。

Kuukua Wilson 绘制的膝关节解剖图显示了这个复杂关节的骨骼、韧带和肌肉。

人们对科学插图有什么误解？

撒哈拉：人们低估了图像背后的劳动。 好的设计不仅需要掌握工具和技术，还需要掌握判断力。 科学插画家的技巧在于设计过程：收集信息的侦探工作、对不同的、有时甚至是相互冲突的来源的综合和评估，以及将复杂的信息转化为图像。 这种视觉问题解决方式既是一种探究模式，也是一种表征模式。

有时，这个过程意味着面对研究尚未解决的问题。 在那些时刻，插图可以成为一种生成知识的方式，而不仅仅是传达知识。

威尔逊：每个人都通过医学书籍、传单或科学杂志中的图像体验过科学插图。 然而大多数人并没有考虑这些来自哪里。

几年前，我与德克萨斯大学合作，为健康素养较低地区接受培训的医生制作伤口视觉效果。 这些需要简单的视觉艺术技术来解释伤口的严重程度何时发生变化、何时进行家庭护理以及何时去诊所。

这既与教育有关，也与插图有关。 医学文献相当复杂；插画家将其分解为需要的人可以访问的图像。 它们通常比文本更容易理解。

侏罗纪时期的海洋鳄鱼 Turnersuchus hingleyae 的草图和最终插图，作者：Júlia d’Oliveira。

侏罗纪时期的海洋鳄鱼 Turnersuchus hingleyae 的草图和最终插图，作者：Júlia d’Oliveira。

你如何处理一个项目？

Wilson：我总是从研究开始，以熟悉该项目。 我创建小草图，以绘制出视觉效果的总体外观和感觉。 然后我和客户进行了一次来回的交流。 他们的意见很重要，因为我不是医学专业人士。 这是一个协作的过程。

Saharan：当然，合作非常重要。 我们不只是创造一个美丽的形象，那不是作品的实质。

每个项目都始于好奇心和持续的探究。 例如，如果我想象一个手术序列，我会收集我能找到的所有东西——论文、录像、解剖参考资料。 如果可以的话，我会去手术室观察手术过程。 然后我与该领域的专家进行一轮又一轮的迭代。

在我们的工作中，你必须把你的自我放在一边，面对你的偏见和假设，并不断质疑它们。 你成为一名不断学习东西的调查员。 您不仅与科学专家密切合作，还与图像的受众密切合作。 最好的插图作品首先是准确的，它解决了特定的沟通挑战。 当严谨与精美、深思熟虑的设计相结合时，这就是真正的力量所在。

一系列显示科学插图的图像显示了两种详细的史前海洋爬行动物。，图像

d’Oliveira：艺术家让事物变得生动起来。 我画灭绝的物种：拥有生物学背景有很大帮助。 我需要知道进化是如何运作的，以及进化联系和谱系方面的系统发育关系是如何运作的。 例如，为了重建恐龙，我不仅使用化石中的信息，还使用与其最近的现存亲戚：鸟类和鳄鱼进行比较。 这将揭示恐龙外观的各个方面，例如鳞片和图案的存在和形状。 然后我需要以受过教育的方式填补空白。 我还阅读了很多地质学知识来想象动物生活的环境。 我写下与插图相关的每个细节，以便我可以回顾它 - 就像科学论文的参考文献一样。

因为我有科学背景，所以我认识地质学家、生物学家和古生物学家，他们可以让我深入了解各自的领域。 每一份工作，我都会学到新的东西。

Weiss：知道如何与科学家交谈至关重要。 我不是天文学家；每个概念都包含一些对我来说是新的视觉元素。 即使在感觉不舒服的时候，提出很多问题也很重要。 如果我不明白我所说明的内容，我将如何帮助其他人这样做？

对天文现象理解的进步也影响了我对宇宙事件和物体的可视化方式。 例如，当天体物理学家深入了解引力透镜（巨大天体周围光线的弯曲）时，我可以创建更周到的黑洞渲染。

在遥远的宇宙中，一些婴儿星系含有黑洞，其质量比根据其宿主星系的大小所预期的要大。 梅丽莎·韦斯（Melissa Weiss）通过展示黑洞（中）在遥远的小星系（左）和附近较大的星系（右）内的外观来描绘这种大小差异。

图片来源：CfA/Melissa Weiss

一系列四个数字艺术工作区屏幕截图，记录了黑洞插图的开发过程，从最初的结构和构图到嵌入螺旋星系中的完整渲染。, 图像

AI对你的工作有影响吗？

Saharan：这项工作并没有像围绕人工智能的对话所暗示的那样发生太大变化——现在很多专业讨论中有一种“采用或死亡”的能量。 我对这两个极端都持怀疑态度。

Marushchenko：对我来说，它改变了科学家启动项目的方式。 当我开始时，这将是一个手绘草图。 这张草图直接反映了研究人员如何看待他们的工作。 如今，它通常是人工智能生成的图像，饱和、光线充足、技术上看起来很自信，但有时在科学上是错误的。

人工智能改变了客户的期望。 人工智能美学已经成为许多研究人员现在推动的默认视觉参考点。 但我们经常为科学期刊创建图像。 当客户要求我们追求人工智能外观时，我们解释说这可能会对他们不利，因为它不符合期刊的要求：清晰度胜过奇观。 有时他们会听。

Júlia d’Oliveira 希望通过使用来自化石描述（左和中）和活鸟（右）的图像来展示鸟类头骨和大脑是如何进化的，首先是草图，然后是最终的渲染。

一系列图像显示了来自不同进化阶段的三个鸟头，剖面图显示了放大且日益复杂的大脑结构。

这些图像基于已灭绝的始祖鸟（左）和纳瓦鸟（中）以及坦加拉属的鸟类（右）。

图片来源：朱莉娅·奥利维拉

您是否使用人工智能工具？如果使用，如何使用？

Weiss：当人工智能首次作为图像制作的来源被引入时，它的不准确性需要与从头开始一样长的时间来纠正。 而且，通过使用它，我失去了对最终结果的部分权威。 我仍然认为，在使用人工智能工具生成原始的、科学上准确的图像时，这是一个主要问题。

许多人工智能工具现在被集成到我用来创建可视化的软件中。 我使用它们就像使用画笔或橡皮擦一样，用于去除小伪影或将背景区域增加几厘米，而不是构建图像的主要基础。 这种程度的使用可以维持我对作品的代理权，并避免对准确性或侵犯版权的担忧。

Saharan：是的，人工智能不仅与道德问题纠缠在一起，还与训练数据的版权、作者身份、责任和同意问题纠缠在一起。 我创建的图像经常传达敏感的、重要的或以前没有被充分可视化的信息。 这种工作所承担的责任是生成式人工智能工具无法代表你承担的。

我探索整个制作流程中的人工智能系统——从研究和构思到视觉制作——以了解它们能做什么和不能做什么。 但是，到目前为止，生成式人工智能工具还没有进入我完成的作品中。 这并不是全然拒绝，而是随着工具及其来源的发展，我不断重新审视的判断。

Marushchenko：我也有类似的情况。 在我创立的工作室中，我们在设计过程中不使用人工智能工具。 我们的团队包括博士级科学家，他们验证所描述的每个结构细节都是准确的。

d’Oliveira：在测试人工智能工具的工作原理时，我发现它们非常擅长渲染看起来逼真的灯光和阴影。 但他们在视角、前景和背景以及准确性方面没有达到目标。 让人工智能工具从骨骼中重新创造出一种动物，比如恐龙，它会生成它所接受的训练：大量糟糕的艺术作品。 它还会从不正确的来源收集信息。

Wilson：我也有意识地努力不在插图或设计中使用任何人工智能工具。 当我收集信息时，我会在项目的早期研究阶段尝试它们。 人工智能可以帮助寻找资源。 但即便如此，我还是更愿意默认使用谷歌学术等搜索引擎来查找同行评审的论文。

Júlia d’Oliveira 描绘的一种飞行爬行动物，称为翼龙 (Tapejara Wellnhoferi)。

Júlia d’Oliveira 描绘的一种飞行爬行动物，称为翼龙 (Tapejara Wellnhoferi)。

Júlia d’Oliveira 绘制的巴西莫图卡地层的插图，该地层保存了以蕨类植物（Grammatopteris）为中心的多种植物群的化石。

图片来源：Júlia d’Oliveira

一幅详细的画作描绘了史前森林场景，其中有各种古老的植物，包括大树蕨和围绕着平静水体的高大针叶树。 该艺术品突出了茂密的植被和多样化的植物结构。, 图片

您认为人工智能无法理解科学插图中的哪些内容？

Marushchenko：人工智能工具将生成几乎所有你向其描述的内容的图像，包括其训练数据中尚不存在的概念。 但它无法验证其产生的结果是否科学准确。 它用视觉上合理的细节填补了空白，而这些细节可能是微妙或实质上错误的。 然而结果看起来很权威：这正是它产生问题的原因。

撒哈拉：在科学插图中，你要对已知的内容、不确定的内容、特定受众的需求以及如何以视觉方式表示这些需求做出决定。 人工智能无法做出这些选择，也无法与临床医生或研究人员合作来协商图像需要传达的内容。 在我看来，这些都是非常人性化、符合情境、充满判断力的行为。

以女性身体为例。 由于长期的排斥、忽视和科学关注不均，可用工作的缺乏。 这段历史也存在于图像中。

今年，我研究了阴蒂和外阴神经支配的解剖学插图——这些结构长期以来一直未被充分研究。 它们是在过去一年才以高分辨率解析并绘制的。 对女性生殖器残割幸存者进行的性别确认手术和重建护理等外科手术最终会切断这些神经。 因此，了解它们在哪里很重要。 人工智能对此无能为力，你无法提示你解决这个问题。

医学历史上忽视某些身体、经历或社区的其他差距包括双性解剖学、性别肯定护理、种族多样性、残疾、慢性疼痛、罕见疾病、衰老和隐形疾病的视觉传达。

Wilson：我们需要创建图像来展示特定条件在一系列肤色上的表现。 人们带着症状去看医生，却被错误地拒绝了，因为医生没有根据他们的肤色识别出他们的病情。

我总是从棕色皮肤开始，因为我有棕色皮肤。 如果我遇到这种病，会是什么样子？ 过去几年，人们的意识有所提高，但实施情况却不尽如人意。 我还看不够。 对于具有局部症状的疾病，创建四到五种不同变体的图像应该是默认的——而不仅仅是我们为一个特定项目所做的事情。

如果人工智能真的要成为一种核心工具，那么教会模型默认使用代表性来源也是有意义的。

不同的领域需要不同的阐释方法吗？

马鲁申科：当然。 这种多样性是我们享受这份工作的重要原因。 每个领域都迫使你以不同的方式思考。 在生物学中，对于科学论文中的人物来说，简单的示意图就可以了，但对于封面来说，最难的部分是让细胞和蛋白质看起来栩栩如生。 你需要正确的纹理、灯光和材料，否则一切看起来都是平淡无奇的。

化学和材料科学是我最喜欢从事的领域之一。 许多分子结构是已知的，因此我们拥有坚实的基础。 从那时起，我和我的同事们开始对如何显示反应、分子相互作用或材料行为发挥创意。 通常，我们被要求可视化一种材料，而我们只有少量的显微镜图像可供使用。 这意味着我们可以将以前从未想象过的东西变为现实。

理论物理学带来了一种不同的自由。 许多现象根本没有物理外观，因此我们不必精确地再现某些东西。 相反，我们设计了一个视觉隐喻来捕捉这个想法。

在过去的一年里，我和我的团队花了几个月的时间为一个神经科学项目研究一个图形。 主题不是一种结构，而是一种模式：大脑如何在状态之间移动，以及它如何位于阈值附近并朝某个方向倾斜。 没有解剖学可以依靠，没有什么可以拍摄——只有一种从动态系统中得出的思维方式，其中行为被描述为不断变化的景观，而不是一组固定的部分。

未来又如何呢？ 您想对崭露头角的插画家说些什么？

d’Oliveira：我不认为科学插画家受到人工智能的威胁。 我认为这对非科学插图来说是一个更大的威胁。

Weiss：我们必须继续学习：作为有创造力的个体，我们需要不断地推动自己进步。 并建立关系。 您建立的联系对您的职业生涯非常重要。

撒哈拉：确实存在一些灾难性的情况，但我想说学生们的担心是适当的。 我们不知道技术将走向何方。

大多数关注点集中在模型有一天可能会做什么，但还有其他方面：尚未解决的版权和所有权问题、这些工具的环境成本以及我们的工艺和我们在该领域的发言权的成本。

我建议学生密切关注人工智能，保持警惕。 需要纪律来抵制那些承诺简单和高效的诱人工具，并反问当创造变得容易时我们可能会失去什么。 对我来说，这才是真正的问题：如何保持有意向。

威尔逊：是的，有一种嗡嗡声的担忧，我认为这是合理的。

当人工智能第一次出现时，它就像是：“这将取代你”。 我们中的许多人立即把它推开了：它会取代我，但首先我需要为它提供我的工作？ 让它有意义。

目前，如果有人想要描绘出正确的解剖结构，他们仍然需要一名医学插画家。 我经常想起硕士期间一位教授说过的话：人们曾经担心插画师会被相机取代。 但如果相机拍摄手术照片，你什么也看不到，只有血​​。 你仍然需要一名医学插画师来绘制其他所有内容。

我预计未来十年会发生类似的事情：仍然需要插画师来澄清、纠正、修改和制作各种观众都能理解的图像。 新来者应该担心，但更多的是要了解足够的人工智能知识，以便能够保持它的位置：它只是一个工具。
